shortExplanation: Nixon denied criminal wrongdoing while evidence of the White House Watergate cover-up and obstruction was emerging.
fullTruth: White House recordings and prosecutions documented obstruction, hush money, and misuse of government power. Nixon resigned in 1974 after the Supreme Court ordered release of the tapes.
context: Rank 88 in the 250-lie source. The excerpt begins ten seconds before the exact denial and ends at the end of the available source video.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies to Nixon's denial of criminal wrongdoing in the documented Watergate context, not to every possible meaning of the word crook.
lesson: A categorical denial should be checked against recordings, conduct, and independent records.

sources.title: Richard Nixon — ‘I'm not a crook’
sources.note: Contains Nixon making the exact denial used in the playable excerpt.

sources.title: Watergate scandal
sources.note: Background evidence for the source-list claim that the Nixon White House denied its role in the cover-up.

shortExplanation: Armstrong repeatedly denied doping before later admitting that he used performance-enhancing drugs throughout his cycling career.
fullTruth: Armstrong was stripped of seven Tour de France titles after the U.S. Anti-Doping Agency documented a sustained doping program. In 2013 he admitted using banned substances during all seven victories.
context: Rank 141 in the 250-lie source. The available source is only twelve seconds long, so the clip uses the full recording while preserving all available context before and after the denial.
transcript: I've said it for longer than seven years. I have never doped.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies to the explicit denial that Armstrong had ever doped.
lesson: Repeated certainty does not outweigh records, witnesses, testing evidence, and a later admission.

media.spokenText: I've said it for longer than seven years. I have never doped.
media.label: Open the complete source recording

sources.note: Contains Armstrong making the exact denial used in the playable excerpt.

sources.title: Lance Armstrong doping case

claim: I don't think there's any question it was the largest watched inauguration ever.
shortExplanation: Spicer defended the claim that Donald Trump's inauguration had the largest audience ever, although photographs, audience measurements, and transit data contradicted the categorical assertion.
fullTruth: The claim combined in-person attendance and global viewing into an unsupported superlative. Available crowd photographs, transit ridership, and television audience data did not establish the largest inauguration audience ever.
context: Rank 238 in the 250-lie source. This excerpt begins ten seconds before the timestamped statement and ends ten seconds after it.
transcript: I don't think there's any question it was the largest watched inauguration ever.
editorialBoundary: The verdict applies to the categorical ‘largest ever’ audience claim, not to the fact that the inauguration was widely watched.
lesson: Superlatives such as largest ever require a defined metric and comparable evidence.

media.spokenText: We have information on so far. I don't think there's any question it was the largest watched inauguration ever.
media.label: Open the complete source recording
